speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so as we went to the house that was burnt out, we split. My-- my father was someplace in the middle of the courtyard. My mother was in a-- step-- in a staircase with my youngest sister. And I and my husband and my-- and my sister, Hana, we were standing by the entrance. All of a sudden, the aeroplane passed by and throw a bomb which fell right in the middle of the courtyards. My father was injured. My mother, I didn't see. And so my grandmother, I didn't see. And I remember only that something would tear me apart like electricity-- like electricity would tear me apart. And that's all I remember. I fell. As I was falling, my sister was next to me. Looks like I was-- automatically grabbed her by her hand and pulled her with me. My husband, somehow he-- he came out from the rubble. And he start searching for me. All of a sudden, he recognized my arm or something. When he pulled me from beneath the dead bodies, I pulled my sister. Because when I fell, I grabbed her by her hand. We looked around. We didn't see my mother, or sister, and my-- my grandmother, or my father, so we ran. That's how we split. When we split, we were running. And then I truly don't remember where we went. I-- I believe we went back to my husband's sister. And my mother and my sister, I don't remember where they went. I only remember that my father went to the place, to [INAUDIBLE] where we used to live.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as he was walking, my neighbor saw him. And he didn't remember anything. She asked him, where is the family? And all he said that they all dead. So she take-- she took him in.

speaker: interviewee
text: She washed him. She took care of his wounds. And she kept him there. Later on, looks like my mother with my two sisters went back home. And she saw my father was in my neighbor's house. We left our grandmother in some room there at the house we were sent. And we were sure that she was killed. But after-- after maybe an hour or so, we were running on the street everybody in each directions. And all of a sudden, I noticed my grandmother. She didn't know where she was going. She was walking in a circle. So we grabbed her. And I say, we thought that you died. And she says, I fell asleep. Because she put her in-- she was already in her late 80s. We put her on the bed. And she says she fell asleep. And she had a dream. Her mother told her to get up and go because she's going to die. She says, I woke up. And she walked out. There was nobody already standing there in the entrance, because everybody-- and she woke up, and she was on the street. But she didn't know where she has to go. So we had our grandmother again. And the whole family met.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- I really don't remember details. But we met. And I remember that we went back to the house to [INAUDIBLE] where we used to live. And as it turned out, that-- on [INAUDIBLE], nothing happened. All the bombing was only in the Jewish-- in the Jewish communities and the Jewish sections. That was the worst bombing. Finally, the Germans marched in. When the Germans marched in, we didn't really have no fear. But maybe a day later, the atrocities started. Warsaw was destroyed terrible. There was no water. There there was no food. There was no electricity. There was no kerosene. There was absolutely nothing. There was a lot of dead horses on the streets. People were running with knives, carving a piece of horse, and cooking. That's how we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I myself didn't. I said, I'll starve to death. I'm not going to eat horse meat. But my gentle neighbor, one day, she-- she invited me. She says she cooked a meal, and she wanted me to come and eat. Being that I was hungry, I went down, and I ate. And it was very good. And I didn't realize that I ate horse meat. Then she-- then she told me that I ate horse meat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you keep kosher?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, nobody thought of keeping anything. There was nothing to eat. Nobody-- we ate whatever we could scavenge. That's what we ate. And so I was with the Germans through-- like, they walked in, maybe in the middle of September or towards the end of September. I don't remember exactly. I lived there then through the end of September, October, November and half of December. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like with the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was very bad. Everything was restricted. Everywhere shivering and shaking. We didn't go outside. Our house was-- our building was-- luckily, our building was standing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe those restrictions further?

speaker: interviewee
text: The restrictions-- not exactly. There was curfew, so you couldn't go on the streets-- only certain hours. The bakery-- when they had the limited-- they used to get a limited amount of flour. So we used to stand in long lines until we got a piece of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: These restrictions applied to everyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: The restrictions applied to everybody. We were scavenging for food because there was a lot of factories that used to make the canned goods. Everything was bombed. So we were constantly looking for food through bombed out factories, through bombed out stores, through things like that. And-- and then a lot of people used to go in the suburb of Warsaw to the farmers to bring some food to sell. So you-- you-- you will manage barely to survive. One day, my husband said, we're going to go to the farmers and see if we could get something to eat. On the way back, I remember we bought some potatoes and some other food. On the way back, I saw some Germans were walking on the streets. And the children were playing. And every-- children will be children. They were playing. And one of the Germans would give him a piece of bread. The other one would run over, grab the piece of bread out of the child's hand, pick him up by his feet, then toss him against the wall.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw that with my own eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think when you saw that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Scared, but I didn't think-- and at that-- at that time, people began-- there began to be rumors that people are going to the other side. The other side meant to be-- to go to the territory occupied by the Russians. That was the other side. And I remember that hundreds of people would go on the road to the other side, that the German airplanes would swoop very, very low. And they used to machine gun them all. And people would come back. So we were scared to leave, and we were scared to be in the house. But one day, that was like, uh, the first days of December, as usually I went with my husband to look for food. As we were coming close to the house, there was a couple of Germans. So just walking in front of us. Somebody saw us that knew us. And turned around to the soldiers, and pointed out to him the two of us. And said, this is a-- a [NON-ENGLISH]. Means 'this is a Jew.' They turned around. They took my husband. They did not touch me. But they took my husband from the side. And they beat him up a little bit. So then they let him go. I was sure they're going to kill him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do while they were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was just standing, because I couldn't move. They did not touch me. They did not approach me, only my husband. And we were almost-- we were maybe three houses away--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see there?

speaker: interviewee
text: --from my house. Oh, it's hard to express what I felt. But I couldn't do nothing. I was just watching. I felt sorry for my husband. And then, when we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you try to express what you felt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, disgust, you know, disgusting, and pity, and fear-- everything combined.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you cry?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not cry. But when we got to the house, my husband said to me, it's time to see if we could escape. I did not want to leave my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what your husband looked like after the beating?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he had a black eye, black cheeks. You know, not all-- not right away, but neck is swollen a little bit. So he-- so he says, we're not going to stay here. If we have to die, we're going to die anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we're going to try. And maybe, we will escape. So we start discussing for leaving the house. Because we hid in our own home, because being that it was before the war, three weeks before the holidays, my parents didn't let us go. So at home, we had two grandmothers, my two sisters, my mother, my father, and we were living there in only one room, in harmony. So when it came to discussion to go, I really cried. I didn't want to leave my parents. So my father said to me, you are married, and you have to go and be with your husband wherever he goes. So I begged my father to let my middle sister, my sister Hana, come with me because she was-- she was, at that time, she was already 13. And you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 18 and a half, quite-- not quite 19. She was about 13 and a half. So my father said to me, your place is with your husband. And her place is with her parents. And he did not let her go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it took-- took another maybe eight days or 10 days. We got a few possessions together just in a backpack. What possessions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, a couple underwear, um, a dress to change, a night gown, and that's about it. And with, you know, whatever we could, a little bit of food, and I don't know how, but it looks like subconsciously, I took out some of the pictures from my album. And my husband says, what are you taking pictures for? And I said, well, if we will survive, I want to have something to remember them by. And if we don't survive, then it doesn't matter where the pictures are going to be. I also took up-- took out a mezuzah from the house from one of the doors. How I did it, I really can't remember. I don't know. I still have it. I gave it to my son.

speaker: interviewee
text: So for about two or three days, we were trying to leave the house. And I just couldn't say goodbye to my parents, to my grandpa-- my-- uh, my grandmothers. Finally, we left. My-- Could you describe how you said goodbye to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very hard. We hugged each other. And, uh, my mother said, go. We blessed you. And maybe that will help [SOB] you survive. Um, my sister, Hana, she said we were supposed to go to the street car, which was on the other-- the other street which called [NON-ENGLISH]. That street car went to the-- like, today would be the Grand Central Station to take a train. So she says, I'm going with you to the street car. She walked with us to the stop sign.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father-- I also said, of course, goodbye to my grandmothers, to my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you tell them? What did your father say to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't really remember what I told them. We left. And many times I tried to remember, I can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your thoughts?

speaker: interviewee
text: I even didn't have any thoughts. I was just going. I really didn't realize then I'll never see them again. I said, I'll see you soon. And I left. I was standing with my sister by the bus stop. We let through probably about 10, 15 street cars. We couldn't part. She was crying. [SIGHS] And she said to me, I know I'll [SOB] never see you again. [SOBBING] And I said-- oh. [DRINKS] And I said to her, yes, you will. It will take maybe a month, two or three. The war will be over, and I come back. And she was right. I never saw her again. Finally, we boarded the street car. And we went to the station. It was early in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your husband say goodbye to his family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he did not say goodbye, because we-- we left his sister. He left his brothers. He left his sister, his brother-in-law, and the children. And we went to our house. But we never had-- we never had the chance to go back there. So we never even did say goodbye. We came to the station. There was German soldiers all over. We were scared. They were going with the guns. And when-- and it was hard to get a ticket to the train. And I don't even remember if we bought any ticket, because when the train came, we were going to the border. And when the trains came, I don't remember who and how they shoved me through the window.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry to interrupt you. Your family, did they want to come with you?

words.text: they want to come with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They couldn't because my father was injured and sick. And they had two old people, two grandmothers. So they couldn't leave. And besides, my father was, you know, with the Germans before. So he didn't realize that this time was different. I even remember, before I left, I just-- when I think about it, I remember when, one day-- also, we went to look for food. And coming home, it was in the Jewish section, you know, like [NON-ENGLISH]. We went there, and I remember I saw an old Jew was walking. And two German soldiers came, approached him, walked in the different direction. And they stopped him. And one took out a knife to cut his beard, and cut half of his face with his beard. And that's when my husband began to say that we have to leave. We have to leave. And I was opposed because I really didn't want to leave my parents and my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I saw that with my own eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. But when my husband was hit by the journalist, that was it. He said it's a breaking point. If you don't want to go, you stay. I'm going. And that's when my father said your place is with your husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said they cut part of his face?

speaker: interviewee
text: Part of his face, and the blood was running. And they were laughing. [INAUDIBLE] sometimes I see all those pictures in front of my eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you-- what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. What can you do? I was 19. They were in the military. They had guns. What could I do? Nothing. So somebody shoved me into the train. And we came to the border. And when we came to the border, I met some of my girlfriends and boyfriends. So we-- we formed like a group. We became a family. When we came to the border--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were about 10 of us. Oh, there were hundreds of people, but my group consisted of friends. We were about 8, 10-- 10 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know they would be there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we met there. So when we came to the border, there was the Germans were standing there on guard. And then you had a strip of land, which they called No Man's Land. And then you had the Russian border. The Germans, they searched you. They-- they-- first of all, they formed three lanes-- one lane, women, one lane, Jews, and one lane, Poles and others. My husband did not look like a Jew, because unless he was at that time pointed out, he had blond hair and blue eyes. And he didn't look like a Jew at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he went to the lane where there was Polish people. I was shivering because I knew that they were going to kill him if they'll find out that he's Jewish. I saw Jews. They ask him, how many shirts you wear? If they say two, and they had six, they would take a gun and kill him right in front of us. You saw that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure, so we were scared. Finally, I saw my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did people react to this?

speaker: interviewee
text: How can you react? You stay in front of soldiers with guns. And you know that they are the enemies. And you-- you hope that every minute you'll be already on No Man's Land, although you didn't know what's-- what's-- what's waiting for you there. Finally, when I saw my husband already passed through the border, they let him go. Because the Poles, they let go. The Jews, they searched. Some of them they let through. Some of them they took away everything. Then the women, when it came to me, I had two, um, I had two pieces of silver with [NON-ENGLISH] on it. It was 10 [NON-ENGLISH] piece. He took one away from me. He says, one for me, one for you. He took away my picture. So I really was-- so I sort of became at ease. And I joked with him. You have to remember I was only 19. I didn't think the way I think now.

speaker: interviewee
text: He took away my picture. And I say, what do you need my picture for? He says, for remembrance. There was good Germans too, but very few, very few. Maybe he was one of them that he felt sorry for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he let us through. We came through the No Man's Land. And there was hundreds of people sitting there because the Russians didn't let you in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the feeling of finally going through?

speaker: interviewee
text: At ease. The only thing I regretted because I saw lots of people sitting there, all the people that came through with whole families, I didn't have nobody-- just my husband and myself. And I was really crying. Why couldn't my family be with me? But it wasn't to be. And we asked people how long they were sitting there. It was snowing. It was raining. Kids were crying. People were sick. The Russians didn't let you in. Now, my husband he was very aggressive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he cry?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we did not cry. He was very aggressive, maybe because he had military training. He went through it also a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he looked around. He let us stay there. And he says, you wait for me. I'm going to look around for the possibilities how we can go through the Russian border.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody pointed out to us that if we'd be able to go through the Russian border, and if for some reason the Russian patrol will stop us, tell them that you want to go to Warsaw. Then they're going to [INAUDIBLE] you back. That's exactly where you want to go. The Russians evacuated all the people by the border. So there was a lot of empty little homes. There was farms, and there were single homes. My husband went to look around. He came back, and he says, I think we have a possibility to escape, not to sit here in No Man's Land. Because people were sitting there. For as long because the war began, they were running.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he told us, all of us, to come as close as possible where the Russians soldiers are. So we did. We were there most-- most of the day, until it became to be a little bit dark, like between day and night. The Russian soldiers, they were talking among themselves. And all of a sudden, they start walking around, walking in the different direction. So their back was turned towards us. So my husband gave a signal. And all of us ran, well, approximately maybe 20 yards. And we ran, and we scattered in the homes. And we sat there until it was a little dark.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my husband gave a signal. He had a special whistle. When he whistled, we came out. And we start walking by the road. When we walked, I can't remember how far-- several kilometers. And we came to the woods. So we stayed in the woods all night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do these woods have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember. And they told us that-- to take a train that goes to Bialystok, which was, before the war, belonged to Poland. But during the war, Russia-- Russia occupied it because they made a pact. The Germans came that far, and the Russian [INAUDIBLE]. That's when they made the-- the strip of No Man's Land.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we-- was a little light, we start walking towards the railroad. When we came there, we waited I can't remember how long until the train came. But by the time the train came, there was thousands of people. Everybody's scared to get in. And somebody, again, shoved me in through the window. I don't even know.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were standing in the train like sardines. None of us knew if the other one got in. We didn't know. I can't remember how long it took until we came to be Bialystok. When we came to Bialystok and the train stopped, and everybody got out, that's when we met. That's when I saw my husband, and I saw my friends. Luckily, everybody got in. So we were marching towards the city. We came into the city. We had--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were still carrying all your possessions with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: All we had-- all I had was a little-- a few things on my back. And the one pair of shoes, that's all we had. When we came to Bialystok, the people were not too friendly. They called us [NON-ENGLISH], you know, like refugee. They sort of were afraid of us. And [COUGHS] so we start asking where there's a concentration of the refugees where we could go. And somebody pointed out to the outskirts of Bialystok. There was a little-- a little village. It looked like it used to be maybe a summer camp or something. There were just shacks. And when we got there, there was a lot of people. So we took up residence. In one of the shacks was 10 of us. And there was nothing. There was just board-- one board from the other was about two inches apart. It was cold. It was snowing. But it was better than staying outside. Then we found out that, once a day, a-- I can't remember who it was-- used to bring hot soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't know. It was in the later part of December, because we left around the middle of December. I don't even remember what the place was called like. So that's-- that night, I remember all the men went to the village, to the farmers, to steal straw. They stole some straw. And they brought it, and they put it up by the wall. And whoever went to sleep first-- we never undressed-- one next to the other. And that's how we lived there, maybe for two weeks. Maybe more, I don't remember exactly. Then somebody came and said that we could register to go to Russia. That they have-- that they're going to have a transport, and they'll take us through Russia. So we did. We registered, and they took us to Russia. They brought us. I can't remember how long we were traveling in those cattle wagons, maybe a week, maybe less. Because it's been so long, I can't remember. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, we were not treated at all. We were on our own. When the train stopped on the station-- which stopped more than was going, because the German's aeroplanes were chasing the transits. So every time the train would stop, everybody would run to the woods. And they were shooting. Some people died right there in front of us. After the aeroplanes used to leave, we used to go back to the-- back to the wagons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any of your friends got hurt?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, all of us came. So it took several days. I can't remember how long. Nobody fed us. When the train stopped on the station someplace, we used to-- mostly the men-- they used to run. Used to run and look for food. We had a little bit money-- a little bit. We took [INAUDIBLE] money, what we could. Finally, they brought us to little-- finally, they brought us to a little town.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to change tapes now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good.
